article_title: Japan Airlines Begins Two-Year Humanoid Robot Trial at Haneda Airport to Address Ground Handling Labor Shortage
summary: Substantively strong reporting on a well-documented story; one unsourced market-leadership claim attributed to Interesting Engineering requires a corrections note.

findings.message: Unitree market-share and location claims not supported by cited source
findings.details: The article states Unitree is 'the highest-volume humanoid seller globally' and attributes this to Interesting Engineering (source-0.html.gz). The Interesting Engineering snapshot does not contain this claim — it describes Unitree only as 'Chinese-made' without any market-share or volume assertion. 'Hangzhou, China-based' is also absent from all five sources. These are subordinate body claims not present in the headline, summary, or lead.

content_preview.title: Japan Airlines Begins Two-Year Humanoid Robot Trial at Haneda Airport to Address Ground Handling Labor Shortage
content_preview.summary: JAL and GMO AI & Robotics deploy Unitree G1 humanoids at Haneda for baggage loading and cabin cleaning, running through 2028.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Japan Airlines has begun a two-year demonstration experiment deploying humanoid robots at Tokyo's Haneda Airport, in a partnership with GMO AI & Robotics Corporation aimed at easing chronic labor shortages in ground handling operations. The trial, which runs from May 2026 through 2028, marks the first time humanoid robots have been used in Japanese airport ground operations and represents a structured test of whether human-shaped machines can take over physically demanding tarmac ta...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 767-word News piece covering all key angles: what the trial involves, the phased rollout plan, labor context, known limitations, and an analytical closing section. Clear, neutral prose throughout. The 'What We Don't Know' section is a notable editorial strength — it honestly acknowledges the two-hour battery constraint and the underwhelming demo video rather than overselling the technology.
editor_notes.source_verification: All five sources successfully snapshotted (source-0 through source-4, all HTTP 200). Sources read: source-0 (Interesting Engineering, Apr 28 2026), source-1 (Engadget, Apr 29 2026), source-2 (Futurism, May 1 2026), source-3 (New Atlas, May 1 2026), source-4 (Robotics and Automation News, Apr 30 2026). Core facts verified: G1 specs (1.32m, 35kg, 7.2km/h, up to 2h battery), $13,500 price (New Atlas), phased trial structure (Engadget), task expansion list (New Atlas), 60M+ Haneda passengers annually (confirmed IE, New Atlas, Futurism), 7M tourists in first two months of 2026 (confirmed IE and Futurism), Uchida quote verbatim (Futurism, original per BBC), Suzuki quote verbatim (Robotics and Automation News), humanoid form-factor rationale (Robotics and Automation News). Issue: The claim 'the highest-volume humanoid seller globally' is attributed to Interesting Engineering but does not appear in source-0. The description of Unitree as 'Hangzhou, China-based' also does not appear in any of the five sources. These appear to be background knowledge injected without source support.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All headline, summary, and lead claims are fully supported by the source snapshots. The single factual issue is a subordinate claim in the 'What We Know' section: the article describes Unitree as 'the Hangzhou, China-based manufacturer whose affordable platforms have made it the highest-volume humanoid seller globally, according to Interesting Engineering' — neither the Hangzhou geography nor the market-leadership assertion appears in the IE snapshot or any other captured source. This can be honestly covered by a corrections note.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism or editorializing. The analysis section correctly frames the Haneda trial as a test case without overstating its significance.
editor_notes.originality: No existing article in the archive covers this story. Topic is original.
concerns: The claim that Unitree is 'the highest-volume humanoid seller globally' is attributed to Interesting Engineering but is absent from the IE snapshot. Similarly, 'Hangzhou, China-based' does not appear in any source. These are subordinate body claims unrelated to the headline or lead, suitable for a corrections note rather than rejection.
recommendations: Future submissions should restrict background manufacturer claims (market share, headquarters city) to facts explicitly stated in one of the cited sources, or add a separate source that supports such claims.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong, well-sourced coverage of a newsworthy aviation robotics deployment. One subordinate unsourced assertion about Unitree's market position warrants an APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS verdict. The rest of the article is accurate and publication-ready.
